summary: Gruber defends his agreement with Jim Cramer's bullish take on Apple stock, addressing reader incredulity given Cramer's track record. He argues that Cramer's earlier sell recommendation was about the tech sector broadly, not Apple specifically, and that being often wrong doesn't preclude being right this time. The core argument is that Apple's dominance among young consumers is a massively underestimated factor that most analysts miss. Gruber points to Apple's remarkable laptop sales growth (61% year-over-year) as evidence of this youth-driven momentum. He contends that Apple's coolness factor among young people, unburdened by outdated Mac perceptions from the '80s and '90s, is what fundamentally separates Apple from the broader tech sector.
key_points: Cramer's January sell recommendation was about the tech sector broadly, and most stocks he warned about did decline — Apple was not the specific target
Apple's dominance among young consumers is both wide (iPods and Macs) and deep (remarkable devotion), and most analysts underestimate it because they themselves are old and uncool
Young consumers lack the negative preconceptions about Macs from the '80s and '90s because anything pre-iPod is prehistoric to them
Apple's laptop sales grew 61% year-over-year versus 37% for desktops, likely driven by student buyers
Apple's youth market strength is precisely what differentiates it from the struggling tech sector as a whole
money_quotes: Even if Cramer is often or even usually wrong about Apple, it doesn't mean he isn't right this time.
The younger one is, the more disproportionately one tends to care about what's cool.
Apple's kit is very cool, and their competitors' kit ranges from 'not cool' to 'totally lame'.
When you're old and everyone around you is doing or buying something you don't understand, you think they're assholes; when you're young, you do it/buy one, too.
Today's students don't have preconceptions (or misconceptions) about Macs dating from the '90s or '80s because anything pre-iPod is prehistoric from their perspective.
Apple's strength in this market is exactly what separates it most from the tech sector as a whole.
key_insight: Apple's iconic status among young consumers — who lack legacy baggage about Macs and care disproportionately about what's cool — is a massively undervalued competitive advantage that most analysts miss because they don't inhabit that market themselves.
